name: Permission Priming Pattern (incl. Permission Ladder)

fields.definition_origin: Permission priming as a formal pattern emerged ~2014-2016 with iOS 8 introducing one-shot permission dialogs (the OS-level dialog could only be shown once per permission per install). Mobile dev community (LaunchKit, Brenden Mulligan blog 2014; OneSignal blog series 2016-2018) codified the 'pre-permission soft ask' to avoid burning the OS prompt. Appcues popularized 'Permission Priming' terminology in their 2018+ playbook. The 'Permission Ladder' (sequencing multiple permissions across the funnel, never asking simultaneously) was articulated by Plotline (2022-2024 blog series), Pushwoosh (push opt-in benchmarks), and the Adapty 2026 onboarding teardown library. Apple iOS 14.5 ATT (App Tracking Transparency, April 2021) made priming MANDATORY for tracking — the soft-ask before ATT dialog became a standard pattern. iOS 26+ (2025) tightened privacy further; pre-permission becomes more critical. Distinct from Pre-Permission Soft-Ask Screen (item 65) — item 24 is the PATTERN/sequencing doctrine; item 65 is the SCREEN artifact.
fields.core_mechanism: Operates via three psychological + technical levers: (1) Reactance avoidance (Brehm 1966) — cold native dialogs feel intrusive and trigger reflexive denial; pre-permission education explains the 'why' before the system framing arrives. (2) Reciprocity (Cialdini 1984) — explaining the user's benefit before asking creates a psychological mini-contract. (3) Technical: iOS allows the native permission dialog to be shown only ONCE per permission per install — denial is permanent (until user manually toggles in Settings). The pre-permission soft-ask creates a 'safe' first ask: if the user says 'Not now' on the soft-ask, you can re-ask later WITHOUT burning the native dialog. (4) Permission ladder: spacing asks across the funnel respects the user's working memory + decision fatigue; cluster-asks (3 permissions back-to-back) crater opt-in. (5) Contextual relevance — asking for camera permission DURING photo capture (not at app launch) leverages just-in-time motivation. The combination dramatically lifts opt-in rates: iOS push opt-in average 43.9% (2025 Pushwoosh data) but climbs to 60-80% with proper priming; Android baseline ~81.5% but priming still adds ~5-10% lift via context.
fields.neuroscience_correlate: Reactance engages anterior cingulate cortex conflict-monitoring + amygdala threat detection. Reciprocity engages medial prefrontal cortex (social decision-making, Sanfey 2007). Just-in-time contextual asks engage curiosity / goal-directed attention systems (Gruber 2014). [uncertain on direct mobile-permission fMRI]
fields.empirical_evidence: (1) Appcues 2024 mobile permission priming benchmark: pre-permission soft-ask lifts iOS push opt-in from ~25-43% baseline to 60-80% across 50+ tracked apps. (2) Pushwoosh 2025 push opt-in benchmark: iOS average 43.9% / Android 81.5%; top quartile (with priming + ladder) hits iOS 70%+ / Android 90%+. (3) Plotline 2024 case studies: permission ladder optimized funnels show 2-3x lift in cumulative permission grants (push + location + ATT) vs cluster-ask flows. (4) Adapty 2026: top-decile apps universally use permission priming; pattern is table stakes. (5) Apple's own iOS 14.5 launch data (paraphrased in WWDC 2022 sessions): app-by-app variability of ATT opt-in 25%-80% almost entirely attributable to priming quality. (6) Branch / AppsFlyer ATT benchmarks 2022-2024: apps with pre-ATT soft-ask average 40-55% opt-in; apps with cold-ATT-on-launch average 15-25%. (7) Brehm reactance theory (1966) replicated 100+ times in social psychology. (8) Apple HIG explicitly recommends pre-permission education for sensitive permissions (camera, microphone, location, photo library, motion, health). (9) PDF Deep Dive: 'Contextless Permissions' is one of Six Antipatterns; permission priming is its explicit counter.
fields.measurable_outcome_metric: Per-permission opt-in rate (iOS push baseline 43.9% target 60-80%; Android push baseline 81.5% target 90%+; ATT baseline 25% target 50%+; location baseline ~50% target 70%+; camera/mic baseline ~70% target 90%+). Funnel-cumulative permission grant rate (% of users who grant ALL desired permissions through the ladder). Permission-deny-then-revisit-Settings rate. Time between permission asks. Per-permission time-to-ask (early in flow vs late).
fields.evidence_strength: industry_benchmark + practitioner_opinion (foundational psychology peer-reviewed; specific app-level priming lift is vendor-published, not peer-reviewed RCT)
fields.conversion_lift_benchmark: Appcues / Pushwoosh / Plotline 2024-2025 benchmarks: pre-permission soft-ask lifts push opt-in 2-3x for iOS (e.g., 25% → 60-70%), 1.1-1.2x for Android (already high baseline). ATT opt-in lift ~2x with priming (15-25% → 40-55%). Location/camera contextual asks at point-of-need lift opt-in ~1.5-2x vs cold launch. Overall permission-ladder strategy lifts cumulative grant rate 2-3x.
fields.engineering_tactics: (1) NEVER ask any permission on cold launch. (2) Define a permission ladder explicitly: PUSH PRIMER (early in onboarding, after value demo, BEFORE paywall typically) → SOFT ASK SHEET → NATIVE DIALOG. Then ATT (separately, after some engagement). Then feature-gated permissions (camera/mic/location/health) at point-of-need. (3) Soft-ask sheet anatomy: full-screen or bottom-sheet modal, value-led headline ('Stay on track with daily reminders' NOT 'Allow notifications'), illustration or screenshot showing the value, primary CTA 'Allow' / 'Enable' (triggers native dialog), secondary 'Maybe later' (defers — does NOT show native dialog). (4) Native dialog ONLY fires after user taps 'Allow' on the soft-ask — protects the one-shot iOS dialog. (5) On 'Maybe later' deferral: schedule contextual re-ask later in flow (e.g., after first action) — re-ask soft-prompt, not native. (6) Track permission state in client (UserDefaults / SharedPreferences) to avoid re-prompting where unnecessary. (7) After native deny: respect — do NOT show soft-ask again unless user takes a clearly-related action (e.g., taps 'Notify me when X' button) and then guide to Settings. (8) ATT specifically: must show iOS native ATT dialog AT SOME POINT to use IDFA; pre-ATT soft-ask should be value-framed ('Show me ads relevant to me' or honest 'Help us make better recommendations'). (9) Spacing: minimum 1 screen between permission asks — never cluster. (10) Order matters: push first (lowest stakes, highest value-to-user), THEN ATT, THEN feature-gated camera/location/health (highest stakes). (11) Telemetry: emit `permission_primer_shown { permission, context }`, `permission_primer_action { permission, action: 'continue' | 'defer' }`, `permission_native_dialog_shown { permission }`, `permission_granted { permission }`, `permission_denied { permission }`, `permission_settings_redirect { permission }`. (12) Post-deny remediation: explicit 'Enable in Settings' card with deep-link to system Settings (UIApplication.openSettingsURLString iOS, equivalent Android Intent).
fields.real_world_examples: (1) Cluster Cluster (canonical OneSignal blog example): pre-push soft-ask 'Get reminded to share photos with family' → native dialog → opt-in lifted from 30% to 75%. (2) Cal AI: push permission ask AFTER AI plan reveal, framed as 'Want daily reminders to log your meals?' — not at cold launch. (3) Headspace: push permission AFTER first meditation completed, framed around session reminders. (4) Strava: location permission contextually asked at first 'Record activity' tap, not at launch. (5) Snapchat: camera permission at first 'Camera' tap (post-account creation), not at launch. (6) Instagram: photo-library permission at first post-creation, not at launch. (7) Apple Mail: pre-permission education before notification dialog explaining VIP filtering. (8) Threads (Meta, 2023 launch): notification permission on second session not first, and only after engagement signal. (9) Mau Baron Prayer Lock: push permission after personalized prayer reveal — framed 'Want your daily prayer at [time]?'. (10) Duolingo: notification ask after first lesson complete, framed around streak protection. (11) Plotline case study (2024): SaaS app moved push ask from cold launch to post-aha — 2.4x opt-in lift. (12) ATT example: TikTok's 2021-2022 ATT soft-ask was widely studied; pre-ATT explanation 'Help us show you relevant content' lifted opt-in materially.
fields.mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile-primary — the permission system is OS-mediated and one-shot in nature on iOS. Web has analog: web push permission (browser-mediated) + cookie consent (site-mediated). Web push opt-in is much lower than mobile (5-15% baseline) because browser dialogs are even more reactance-triggering. Web-equivalent: pre-permission custom modal before browser push request, geo-aware CMP for cookie consent (Axeptio, OneTrust, Usercentrics). Web has no ATT analog but EU CMP requirements parallel.
fields.platform_design_language_alignment: Liquid Glass (iOS 26+): bolder, left-aligned alert typography is purpose-built for the soft-ask sheet — value-led headline reads beautifully. iOS soft-ask sheet should use system materials (UIBlurEffect) to feel native. M3 Expressive (Android 16+): expressive bottom-sheet primitives align with soft-ask pattern; Android's higher baseline opt-in means soft-ask less critical but contextual placement still helps. Both DLs: soft-ask must feel native, not foreign.
fields.category_fit: Universal — every category has at least push permission to ask. Category-tuned: wellness/habit apps lean heavy on push primer (notifications are the engagement engine); social apps lean push + photo-library + camera + contacts; fitness/maps lean location + motion; AI-camera apps lean camera + mic + photo-library; fintech leans biometrics + face ID + location for fraud detection. EXCLUSION-ish: utilities with no notification need can skip push primer entirely — don't ask for permissions you don't need (data minimization, item 46).
fields.solo_dev_feasibility: 1_week. Building the soft-ask sheet primitive is 1-2 days; permission ladder integration in FSM is 1-2 days; copy authoring + variant testing rest of the week. Plotline / OneSignal Deferred Prompt provide hosted versions reducing time to a day. Critical to do this RIGHT because every permission denial is permanent (iOS) or near-permanent (Android prompts limited).
fields.tooling_options: Plotline (mobile-native deferred-prompt + permission flow), OneSignal Deferred Prompt, Pushwoosh (push management + priming), Iterable (push + email + priming), Adapty Onboarding Builder (incorporates permission ladder), Custom build using native APIs (UNUserNotificationCenter iOS, NotificationManagerCompat Android). For ATT: Apple's AppTrackingTransparency framework + custom soft-ask sheet. For location: CoreLocation iOS, FusedLocationProvider Android.
fields.ai_dependency: none. Permission priming is structural UX. Optional: AI-generated soft-ask copy variants for A/B testing (small classifier model can generate variants based on user persona / questionnaire answers).
fields.claude_design_application: Instruct Claude Design to build the permission ladder as an explicit FSM with soft-ask sheet primitive. Prompt fragment: 'Build a PermissionLadder component as XState v5 states: ask_push (soft-ask sheet → native dialog) → ask_att (soft-ask sheet → native ATT dialog) → contextual_camera_at_first_use → contextual_location_at_first_use → contextual_health_at_first_use. NEVER fire native dialog directly. Each soft-ask sheet anatomy: bottom-sheet modal (320pt min height), illustrative SVG or screenshot at top (NOT Lucide icon), value-led headline ("Stay on track with daily reminders" — NEVER "Allow notifications"), 1-line body explaining benefit, primary CTA "Enable" (triggers native), secondary "Maybe later" text-link (defers without burning native). Spacing: minimum 1 screen between permission asks. Order: push → ATT → feature-gated. On native deny, respect — do not re-ask, but provide a Settings deep-link card on the relevant feature surface (e.g., notifications-disabled banner with "Open Settings" button). Wire telemetry: permission_primer_shown / action / native_dialog_shown / granted / denied. Forbid: cold-launch native dialogs, clustered permission asks, generic Allow / Don\'t Allow framing.'
fields.stitch_application: Prompt fragment: 'Mockup an iOS push permission soft-ask sheet at 390x844. Bottom sheet, 60% screen height, white background, 16px top radius. Top: a illustrated bell-with-soft-glow image (NOT Lucide bell icon, NOT iOS system bell) 64x64px, centered, 32px top inset. Headline (32px Cabinet Grotesk Semibold, centered, max 6 words): "Stay on track daily." Body (15px Söhne Regular, centered, 2 lines max): "We\'ll remind you at the time you choose. Skip anytime." Spacer. Primary CTA (full-width, 56px tall, 16px horizontal inset, brand accent #E36B2C): "Enable Notifications". Secondary tertiary text-link below CTA, centered, 15px Söhne Regular: "Maybe later". Forbid: Lucide bell icon, generic "Allow" / "Don\'t Allow" wording, system iOS modal styling (this is a CUSTOM soft-ask before the native dialog), indigo gradient.'
fields.ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI codegen typically generates a cold-launch chain of native permission dialogs immediately on app open — the canonical 'Contextless Permissions' antipattern (Six-Antipattern #4). Specific failures: (1) push, location, contacts, camera all asked on first launch back-to-back, (2) no soft-ask before native dialog (burns the one-shot), (3) generic 'Allow' framing instead of value-led ('Stay on track' / 'Find friends nearby'), (4) no contextual placement — camera permission at launch instead of at first photo, (5) no post-deny remediation (no Settings deep-link), (6) no ATT priming (cold ATT dialog), (7) no telemetry on the priming layer. Counter-prompt: 'CONSTRAINT: NEVER fire a native permission dialog without a custom soft-ask sheet first. Define a permission ladder FSM with at least 1 screen between asks. Order: push primer → ATT primer → feature-gated camera/location/etc at first use. Soft-ask copy must be value-led (NOT "Allow notifications"). On "Maybe later" tap, do NOT show the native dialog — defer for contextual re-ask. On native deny, do NOT re-prompt; provide Settings deep-link card on feature surface. Forbid: cold-launch permission asks, clustered permission asks, generic Allow framing, missing post-deny remediation. Wire telemetry on every step.'
fields.anti_patterns: (1) Contextless Permissions (Six-Antipattern #4) — cold native dialog without priming. (2) Cluster-ask — 3 permissions in 30 seconds. (3) Generic value-framing ('Allow notifications' instead of 'Stay on track with reminders'). (4) Burning the iOS one-shot — firing native dialog before soft-ask, then user denies, permanent. (5) Re-asking after native deny (cannot — must guide to Settings). (6) Missing post-deny remediation (no Settings deep-link). (7) Push primer before any value demonstrated (asks too early in flow). (8) Permissions you don't actually need (collect-everything mindset). (9) Permission framings that sound like sales ('Get exclusive offers' for push). (10) No ATT priming — cold ATT dialog. (11) Re-asking 'Maybe later' users every screen. (12) Permission soft-ask that closes onto the same screen with no progress (gives up on the user).
fields.regulatory_risk: Medium-High. GDPR Article 7 valid consent — must be freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous; permission soft-asks that pressure or mislead violate. ePrivacy Directive (2002, 2009 amended) requires explicit consent for non-essential tracking — covers push and analytics in EU. ATT specifically subject to GDPR — Apple's framework alone doesn't satisfy GDPR; combine with proper CMP. EU Digital Fairness Act (Q4 2026 draft) likely to flag manipulative permission soft-asks (false-urgency, dark-pattern wording). UK ICO 'consent or pay' guidance (2024) for pay-walled tracking. Misleading permission copy = FTC § 5 deceptive practice exposure. Apple App Store Guideline 5.1.1 (Data Collection and Storage): permission usage strings must accurately describe purpose. Android Play Data Safety form must mirror.
fields.platform_constraints_2026: Apple Guideline 5.1.1 — every permission must have an accurate Info.plist usage description; rejection if misleading. Apple ATT framework (iOS 14.5+) mandatory for IDFA tracking. Apple iOS 14.5+: notification permission denial is permanent (Settings re-enable only). iOS 18+ added App Privacy Report enhancements. iOS 26+ (2025): privacy enforcement tightened. Android target API 35 (2025-08-31) Privacy Sandbox + Permissions API — runtime permissions for sensitive groups. Both: data minimization principle (collect only what's needed for declared functionality) is enforced. EU EAA 2025 + UK Online Safety Act add accessibility and child-safety overlays.
fields.accessibility_implications: WCAG 2.2: soft-ask sheet must have semantic heading, accessible CTA labels (NOT 'Tap here'), VoiceOver focus moves to sheet on appearance and back to underlying screen on dismissal. Native iOS/Android permission dialogs are accessibility-tested by the OS — soft-ask sheets must match. Dynamic Type 200%: sheet content must reflow without truncation; do NOT cap headlines at fixed pixel height. prefers-reduced-motion: disable sheet entry animation, use crossfade. Color contrast: soft-ask CTAs must meet 4.5:1 minimum.
fields.pitfalls_caveats: (1) iOS one-shot: burning the native dialog is permanent damage. Soft-ask MUST gate every native ask. (2) Cross-platform parity: Android push opt-in is much higher than iOS, but priming still helps; don't skip Android primer just because baseline is high. (3) Apple's review team scrutinizes permission usage strings — generic strings get rejected. (4) ATT timing matters — too early in flow (no engagement) tanks opt-in; too late wastes attribution data. Best fit: after first engagement signal, before any 'monetization moment'. (5) Permission ladder localization — copy must be locale-authored; translate-only loses value-framing nuance. (6) Re-ask logic on 'Maybe later' is fragile — must track timing carefully to avoid re-asking on every screen. (7) Health permission (HealthKit) requires explicit per-data-type request; bundle carefully. (8) Background location triggers Apple/Google scrutiny — only ask if essential, with strong justification.
fields.cross_cultural_validity: Generally universal but copy register and value framing vary. JP/KR: more polite language for the soft-ask ('We would like to send you reminders' vs imperative 'Stay on track'). LATAM: warmer/family-context framing ('Tu familia recibirá actualizaciones'). MENA: faith/community context where relevant. CN: super-app push behavior is different (in-app notifications dominate over OS push). EU: GDPR compliance overlays must be present alongside permission priming — pre-permission soft-ask + cookie consent + ATT layered. India: WhatsApp/SMS preference over push in many segments — consider deferring push primer for India locale.
fields.prescribed_action: Define an explicit permission ladder: push primer → push native → ATT primer → ATT native → feature-gated camera/location/health at point-of-first-use. NEVER fire any native dialog without a custom soft-ask sheet first. Soft-ask copy must be value-led, NOT 'Allow notifications'. Spacing: minimum 1 screen between permission asks. On 'Maybe later' tap, defer for contextual re-ask — do NOT show native. On native deny, respect — provide Settings deep-link card on relevant feature surface. Wire full telemetry on every permission step.
fields.questionnaire_relevance: Permission ladder lives ALONGSIDE questionnaire — push primer typically fires after first meaningful answer (mid-questionnaire); ATT primer typically fires post-AI-reveal pre-paywall; feature-gated permissions fire post-onboarding at first feature use. Coordinate placement with questionnaire architecture (item 26 sequencing).
fields.aha_relevance: Permission grants enable the engagement loops that make subsequent Aha moments possible. Push permission specifically protects the Day-1/2 retention push (item 59). Asking before Aha (cold) tanks opt-in; asking just after the AI Plan Reveal Aha (item 12) typically performs best — engagement is high, value just demonstrated.
fields.measurement_horizon: Day_0 (per-permission opt-in rate) + Day_7 (push delivery rate, feature usage rate conditioned on permissions)
fields.emotional_archetype_alignment: Sage / Caregiver (clear, transparent, value-led copy aligns naturally — 'Here's why we ask'). Magician (frame permission as enabling the magic — 'Let us send you your daily prayer at the right time'). Hero (frame permission as enabling progress — 'Don't lose your streak'). Mismatch: Outlaw (permission asks read paternalistic; minimize the count, lean opt-in only when feature-needed). Jester (playful copy works but maintain clarity).
fields.anti_pattern_paired: Pairs against 'Contextless Permissions' (Six-Antipattern #4) — explicit counter. Pairs against 'Wall-of-Text + Cognitive Overload' (#1) when soft-ask copy is too long. Pairs against 'No-Escape Room' (#3) when permission asks have no defer affordance.
sources: [How to Increase Push Notifications Opt-in Rates — Pushwoosh](https://www.pushwoosh.com/blog/increase-push-notifications-opt-in/)
[How to Improve Push Notification Opt-in Rates — Plotline](https://www.plotline.so/blog/how-to-improve-push-notification-opt-in-rates)

uncertain: specific RCT-grade lift figures per permission type per category (vendor benchmarks are aggregated)
